skill.name: test-scaffolding
skill.description: Generate language-aware test scaffolds from source files and project manifests for common test frameworks.
skill.summary: Create TODO-based test scaffold files for Python, TypeScript, Go, Rust, and Dart projects.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are documentation and template examples, not executable skill code. The skill does read source files and can generate test files, so the remaining risk is limited to normal filesystem changes during scaffold creation.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Test Scaffold Generation
low_findings.description: The skill is designed to read source files, inspect project manifests, and create test files. This is legitimate for its purpose, but users should review generated file paths before writing into a repository.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly reads manifests, analyzes sources, maps test paths, and generates scaffolds. The behavior is expected for the skill and includes safeguards for existing files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The external command findings are false positives caused by markdown inline code and fenced examples. The reviewed files contain regex patterns, file names, and test templates, not Ruby backtick execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown fences or inline examples. No executable Ruby source or shell command invocation is present in the reviewed context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Import Syntax Misclassified as Dynamic Script Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The script findings are false positives. The cited Go template lines show normal Go import blocks inside documentation examples, not JavaScript dynamic import expressions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code is fenced Go template content and only imports the standard testing package. There is no dynamic module loading behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Template Import Path Misclassified as Path Traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: The filesystem path traversal findings are false positives. The TypeScript templates show relative import path examples, not filesystem reads or writes using attacker-controlled traversal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The ../ text appears in an import path example for generated tests. It is not used in executable path handling code in the skill files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Misclassified in Test Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography findings are false positives. The cited lines are headings, markdown table rows, or ordinary test template statements, with no hashing or encryption implementation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no MD5, SHA-1, or crypto usage at the cited locations. The static category does not match the file content.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time creating repetitive test files for each source module. This skill generates framework-specific scaffold files with TODO stubs, fixture placeholders, and naming conventions.
actual_capabilities: Detects common project manifests such as pyproject.toml, package.json, go.mod, Cargo.toml, and pubspec.yaml.
Maps Python, TypeScript, Go, Rust, and Dart source files to language-specific test locations.
Extracts public functions, classes, methods, structs, and exported symbols for scaffold planning.
Generates TODO-based test stubs for pytest, Jest, Vitest, Go testing, cargo test, and flutter_test.
Returns a scaffold manifest with generated files, skipped files, unit names, and unit counts.
Includes fixture, mock, async, parameterized, and table-driven template patterns where supported.
limitations: Generated tests are scaffolds only and still require real assertions and test data.
Framework detection depends on project manifests and may need user input when manifests are missing.
Source analysis uses documented heuristics and may miss complex metaprogramming or generated symbols.
Existing test files are skipped unless the user explicitly allows replacement.

use_cases.title: Start TDD for a New Feature
use_cases.description: Create empty test files and TODO cases before implementation work begins.

use_cases.title: Backfill Tests for Existing Modules
use_cases.description: Analyze public symbols and generate missing scaffold files for legacy source code.

use_cases.title: Standardize Multi-Language Test Layouts
use_cases.description: Apply consistent naming and framework conventions across Python, TypeScript, Go, Rust, and Dart projects.

prompt_templates.title: Scaffold Basic Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one source file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the test-scaffolding skill for src/auth/login.py. Detect the framework and create TODO-based test scaffolds.

prompt_templates.title: Scaffold Multiple Files
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate tests for several related modules.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use test-scaffolding for src/auth/login.ts, src/auth/session.ts, and src/auth/tokens.ts. Preserve the source directory structure in the test files.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Specific Framework
prompt_templates.prompt: Use test-scaffolding with TEST_FRAMEWORK=vitest for these TypeScript files. Include async and mock template sections where useful.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Lane Manifest
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow integration with implementation planning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use test-scaffolding for the owned artifacts in this implementation lane. Return the scaffold manifest with generated paths, skipped files, and unit counts.

output_examples.input: A Python authentication module with a session class and login functions.
output_examples.output: A pytest scaffold with a class test group, fixture placeholder, function test stubs, TODO comments, and skipped existing files listed in the manifest.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript service file using Vitest in package.json.
output_examples.output: A Vitest test file beside the source file, with imports, describe blocks, async test placeholders, and mock setup guidance.

output_examples.input: A Go package with exported functions and receiver methods.
output_examples.output: A _test.go scaffold in the same package, with Test functions, subtests for happy paths and edge cases, and table-driven test placeholders.

best_practices: Review generated file paths before writing scaffolds into a repository.
Replace TODO placeholders with real assertions and realistic fixtures before relying on the tests.
Run the project test command after filling scaffolds to confirm imports, naming, and framework configuration.
anti_patterns: Do not publish scaffold files that still contain failing placeholders as completed tests.
Do not force overwrite existing test files without reviewing local changes first.
Do not use the heuristic output as complete coverage analysis for complex or generated code.

faq.question: Does this skill write complete tests?
faq.answer: No. It creates structured scaffolds with TODO placeholders that developers or agents must complete.

faq.question: Which languages are supported?
faq.answer: It documents support for Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Go, Rust, and Dart test scaffolds.

faq.question: Which test frameworks can it target?
faq.answer: It supports pytest, unittest, Jest, Vitest, Go testing, cargo test, flutter_test, and Dart test patterns.

faq.question: How does it choose file names?
faq.answer: It maps source files to framework-specific test paths, such as test_login.py, login.test.ts, or login_test.go.

faq.question: Will it overwrite existing tests?
faq.answer: The documented workflow skips existing test files unless a force option is explicitly used.

faq.question: Can it integrate with agent workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents integration with planning, lane execution, and a test-engineer agent.
